tools.name: configuration_view
tools.description: Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML

minified.description: Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)

tools.name: events_list
tools.description: List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter events by field values (e.g. 'type=Warning', 'involvedObject.name=my-pod'). Supported fields: involvedObject.kind, involvedObject.name, involvedObject.namespace, involvedObject.uid, involvedObject.apiVersion, involvedObject.resourceVersion, involvedObject.fieldPath, reason, reportingComponent, source, type. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces

tools.name: namespaces_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter namespaces by field values (e.g. 'metadata.name=default', 'status.phase=Active'). Supported fields: metadata.name, status.phase. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

tools.name: nodes_log
tools.description: Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet

name.description: Name of the node to get logs from

query.description: query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")

tailLines.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)

tools.name: nodes_stats_summary
tools.description: Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics

name.description: Name of the node to get stats from

tools.name: nodes_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)

tools.name: pods_delete
tools.description: Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

name.description: Name of the Pod to delete

namespace.description: Namespace to delete the Pod from

tools.name: pods_exec
tools.description: Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command

command.description: Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]

container.description: Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod where the command will be executed

namespace.description: Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed

tools.name: pods_get
tools.description: Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod from

tools.name: pods_list
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_list_in_namespace
tools.description: List all the Kubernetes pods in the specified namespace in the current cluster

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label

tools.name: pods_log
tools.description: Get the logs of a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name

container.description: Name of the Pod container to get the logs from (Optional)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the logs from

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pod logs from

tail.description: Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, default: 100)

tools.name: pods_run
tools.description: Run a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided container image and optional name

image.description: Container Image to run in the Pod

name.description: Name of the Pod (Optional, random name if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to run the Pod in

port.description: TCP/IP port to expose from the Pod container (Optional, no port exposed if not provided)

tools.name: pods_top
tools.description: List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Pods in the all namespaces, the provided namespace, or the current namespace

all_namespaces.description: If true, list the resource consumption for all Pods in all namespaces. If false, list the resource consumption for Pods in the provided namespace or the current namespace

label_selector.description: Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the pods by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)

name.description: Name of the Pod to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Pods in the namespace if not provided)

namespace.description: Namespace to get the Pods resource consumption from (Optional, current namespace if not provided and all_namespaces is false)

tools.name: resources_create_or_update
tools.description:
Create or update a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing a YAML or JSON representation of the resource
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

resource.description: A JSON or YAML containing a representation of the Kubernetes resource. Should include top-level fields such as apiVersion,kind,metadata, and spec

tools.name: resources_delete
tools.description:
Delete a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

gracePeriodSeconds.description: Optional duration in seconds before the object should be deleted. Value must be non-negative integer. The value zero indicates delete immediately. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified type will be used

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to delete the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will delete resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_get
tools.description:
Get a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, optionally the namespace, and its name
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resource from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get resource from configured namespace

tools.name: resources_list
tools.description:
List Kubernetes resources and objects in the current cluster by providing their apiVersion and kind and optionally the namespace and label selector
(common apiVersion and kind include: v1 Pod, v1 Service, v1 Node, apps/v1 Deployment, networking.k8s.io/v1 Ingress)

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resources (examples of valid apiVersion are: v1, apps/v1, networking.k8s.io/v1)

fieldSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter resources by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'metadata.name=myresource'). Supported fields vary by resource type. For Pods: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/field-selectors/

kind.description: kind of the resources (examples of valid kind are: Pod, Service, Deployment, Ingress)

labelSelector.description: Optional Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'app=myapp,env=prod' or 'app in (myapp,yourapp)'), use this option when you want to filter the resources by label

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to retrieve the namespaced resources from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will list resources from all namespaces

tools.name: resources_scale
tools.description: Get or update the scale of a Kubernetes resource in the current cluster by providing its apiVersion, kind, name, and optionally the namespace. If the scale is set in the tool call, the scale will be updated to that value. Always returns the current scale of the resource

apiVersion.description: apiVersion of the resource (examples of valid apiVersion are apps/v1)

kind.description: kind of the resource (examples of valid kind are: StatefulSet, Deployment)

namespace.description: Optional Namespace to get/update the namespaced resource scale from (ignored in case of cluster scoped resources). If not provided, will get/update resource scale from configured namespace

scale.description: Optional scale to update the resources scale to. If not provided, will return the current scale of the resource, and not update it

results.taskOutput:
Done.

What I changed
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext; pod-level securityContext: {})
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to target both deployments
  - Selector: app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint)
  - Ports left unchanged (port 80 -> targetPort 8080 as before)

Result
- Pods now behind the Service:
  - Stable: 2 pods (version=v2.0)
  - Canary: 2 pods (version=v2.1)
- With equal replicas, traffic will be distributed about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current pods
- engine-v2-0-… (2 pods) Running
- engine-v2-1-… (2 pods) Running

Notes
- Traffic split is replica-weighted. To adjust, scale either Deployment.
- Rollback options:
  - Point Service back to stable only by setting selector to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0
  - Or scale canary to 0 replicas.

namespace/canary-deployment-ns created
deployment.apps/engine-v2-0 created
service/recommendation-engine created

Done.

What I changed
- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext; pod-level securityContext: {})
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to target both deployments
  - Selector: app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint)
  - Ports left unchanged (port 80 -> targetPort 8080 as before)

Result
- Pods now behind the Service:
  - Stable: 2 pods (version=v2.0)
  - Canary: 2 pods (version=v2.1)
- With equal replicas, traffic will be distributed about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current pods
- engine-v2-0-… (2 pods) Running
- engine-v2-1-… (2 pods) Running

Notes
- Traffic split is replica-weighted. To adjust, scale either Deployment.
- Rollback options:
  - Point Service back to stable only by setting selector to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0
  - Or scale canary to 0 replicas.

- Created canary Deployment engine-v2-1 (namespace canary-deployment-ns)
  - Image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:1.29
  - Replicas: 2
  - Labels: app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.1
  - Container: rec-engine, containerPort: 80
  - Security config: same as stable (no explicit securityContext; pod-level securityContext: {})
- Updated Service recommendation-engine to target both deployments
  - Selector: app=recommendation-engine (removed version constraint)
  - Ports left unchanged (port 80 -> targetPort 8080 as before)

Result
- Pods now behind the Service:
  - Stable: 2 pods (version=v2.0)
  - Canary: 2 pods (version=v2.1)
- With equal replicas, traffic will be distributed about 50/50 across the 4 endpoints.

Current pods
- engine-v2-0-… (2 pods) Running
- engine-v2-1-… (2 pods) Running

Notes
- Traffic split is replica-weighted. To adjust, scale either Deployment.
- Rollback options:
  - Point Service back to stable only by setting selector to app=recommendation-engine, version=v2.0
  - Or scale canary to 0 replicas.

results.taskOutput:
Done. I created:
- Namespace: color-size-settings
- ConfigMaps:
  - color-settings with data color=blue
  - size-settings with data size=medium
- Pod: pod1 (image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:alpine) in namespace color-size-settings
  - Env var COLOR is sourced from color-settings key color
  - ConfigMap size-settings is mounted at /etc/sizes/ (all keys as files)

To verify once the pod is running:
- Wait for readiness:
  - kubectl wait -n color-size-settings --for=condition=ready pod/pod1 --timeout=120s
- Check env var:
  - kubectl -n color-size-settings exec pod/pod1 -- printenv COLOR
- Check mounted files:
  - kubectl -n color-size-settings exec pod/pod1 -- sh -lc "ls -l /etc/sizes && cat /etc/sizes/size"

results.taskOutput:
Short answer: nothing is wrong with the Pod from Kubernetes’ point of view; it’s Running and Ready with 0 restarts. The “problem” is in the app code.

What’s happening
- The container is healthy (phase: Running, Ready=True).
- The app logs show frequent “division by zero” errors. Your ConfigMap script intentionally does this:
  - It triggers a ZeroDivisionError about 25% of the time and catches it, printing “failed with error: division by zero.”
  - It then keeps running, so the pod doesn’t crash.
- The sleep time grows exponentially (sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20), so logs slow down dramatically over time, which can make it look stuck.

How to fix or adjust
- Remove the intentional division by zero. For example, replace that block with a safe operation or just log a simulated failure without raising:
  - Use if random.random() < 0.25: to simulate a 25% event, and don’t compute 1/0.
- Tame the sleep to avoid long pauses:
  - Use a constant sleep (e.g., time.sleep(0.5)) or cap it (e.g., sleep_time = min(2, (1.2**counter)/20)).
- Apply the updated ConfigMap and restart the Pod to pick up changes:
  - kubectl -n calc-app apply -f your-configmap.yaml
  - Then restart the Pod so python rereads the file:
    - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod
    - If this is a standalone Pod, re-apply its manifest after deletion.

Optional hardening
- Add resource requests/limits to avoid BestEffort QoS.
- Consider running as non-root via securityContext if required by your policies.
results.taskJudgeReason: Pass under CONTAINS criterion: The model response explicitly identifies the issue as “division by zero” occurring in the app (ZeroDivisionError), which fully includes the core semantic content of the reference answer. Additional details (frequency, config, remediation) are extra but non-contradictory.

    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

{"content":[{"type":"text","text":"apiVersion: v1
data:
  calc-app.py: |
    import random
    import sys
    import time

    print("Starting calc-app...")
    sys.stdout.flush()

    counter = 0
    while True:
        counter += 1
        if random.randint(1, 4) % 4 == 0:
            try:
                result = 1 / 0
            except ZeroDivisionError as e:
                print(f"Run {counter} failed with error: {e}")
                sys.stdout.flush()
        else:
            print(f"Run {counter} succeeded")
            sys.stdout.flush()

        sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20
        time.sleep(sleep_time)

outputSteps.content:
Short answer: nothing is wrong with the Pod from Kubernetes’ point of view; it’s Running and Ready with 0 restarts. The “problem” is in the app code.

What’s happening
- The container is healthy (phase: Running, Ready=True).
- The app logs show frequent “division by zero” errors. Your ConfigMap script intentionally does this:
  - It triggers a ZeroDivisionError about 25% of the time and catches it, printing “failed with error: division by zero.”
  - It then keeps running, so the pod doesn’t crash.
- The sleep time grows exponentially (sleep_time = (1.2**counter)/20), so logs slow down dramatically over time, which can make it look stuck.

How to fix or adjust
- Remove the intentional division by zero. For example, replace that block with a safe operation or just log a simulated failure without raising:
  - Use if random.random() < 0.25: to simulate a 25% event, and don’t compute 1/0.
- Tame the sleep to avoid long pauses:
  - Use a constant sleep (e.g., time.sleep(0.5)) or cap it (e.g., sleep_time = min(2, (1.2**counter)/20)).
- Apply the updated ConfigMap and restart the Pod to pick up changes:
  - kubectl -n calc-app apply -f your-configmap.yaml
  - Then restart the Pod so python rereads the file:
    - kubectl -n calc-app delete pod calc-app-pod
    - If this is a standalone Pod, re-apply its manifest after deletion.

Optional hardening
- Add resource requests/limits to avoid BestEffort QoS.
- Consider running as non-root via securityContext if required by your policies.

Steps.message: Pass under CONTAINS criterion: The model response explicitly identifies the issue as “division by zero” occurring in the app (ZeroDivisionError), which fully includes the core semantic content of the reference answer. Additional details (frequency, config, remediation) are extra but non-contradictory.

results.taskOutput:
Fixed.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command to run “/bin/sh -c nonexistent_command”, which doesn’t exist, causing CrashLoopBackOff. Pod log: “/bin/sh: 1: nonexistent_command: not found”.
- Fix applied: Removed the custom command/args override so the container uses the image’s default entrypoint (nginx).
- Current status: One replica is Running and Ready.
  - Pod: app-785548454c-lcq5z in crashloop-test is 1/1 Running. The old crashing pod was terminated as the ReplicaSet rolled over.

If you actually need a custom command, replace it with a valid one (for example, command: [“/bin/sh”, “-c”], args: [“nginx -g ‘daemon off;’”]).

Steps.message:
Fixed.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command to run “/bin/sh -c nonexistent_command”, which doesn’t exist, causing CrashLoopBackOff. Pod log: “/bin/sh: 1: nonexistent_command: not found”.
- Fix applied: Removed the custom command/args override so the container uses the image’s default entrypoint (nginx).
- Current status: One replica is Running and Ready.
  - Pod: app-785548454c-lcq5z in crashloop-test is 1/1 Running. The old crashing pod was terminated as the ReplicaSet rolled over.

If you actually need a custom command, replace it with a valid one (for example, command: [“/bin/sh”, “-c”], args: [“nginx -g ‘daemon off;’”]).

kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: app
  namespace: crashloop-test
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged
        imagePullPolicy: Always

outputSteps.content:
Fixed.

- Root cause: The Deployment overrode the container command to run “/bin/sh -c nonexistent_command”, which doesn’t exist, causing CrashLoopBackOff. Pod log: “/bin/sh: 1: nonexistent_command: not found”.
- Fix applied: Removed the custom command/args override so the container uses the image’s default entrypoint (nginx).
- Current status: One replica is Running and Ready.
  - Pod: app-785548454c-lcq5z in crashloop-test is 1/1 Running. The old crashing pod was terminated as the ReplicaSet rolled over.

If you actually need a custom command, replace it with a valid one (for example, command: [“/bin/sh”, “-c”], args: [“nginx -g ‘daemon off;’”]).

results.taskOutput:
I fixed the deployment.

What was wrong
- The image in Deployment debug/app was quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag, which doesn’t exist. Pods were failing with ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff.
- My first correction to quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:latest hit 401 UNAUTHORIZED (requires auth).

What I changed
- Updated the deployment to use a public, pullable image:
  - container nginx image: docker.io/library/nginx:alpine

Current status
- Deployment debug/app is healthy and Available=True.
- 1/1 replicas ready; Pod app-59c9bb5f58-mmxh6 is Running.

If you prefer the unprivileged NGINX image
- Use a public tag that doesn’t require auth, e.g. quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable-alpine (or a specific version), or
- Configure imagePullSecrets for your registry credentials and use your desired private image/tag.

Verify
- kubectl -n debug rollout status deploy/app
- kubectl -n debug get deploy app
- kubectl -n debug get pods -l app=nginx

Want me to switch to a specific image/tag or add imagePullSecrets for a private registry?

Steps.message:
I fixed the deployment.

What was wrong
- The image in Deployment debug/app was quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag, which doesn’t exist. Pods were failing with ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff.
- My first correction to quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:latest hit 401 UNAUTHORIZED (requires auth).

What I changed
- Updated the deployment to use a public, pullable image:
  - container nginx image: docker.io/library/nginx:alpine

Current status
- Deployment debug/app is healthy and Available=True.
- 1/1 replicas ready; Pod app-59c9bb5f58-mmxh6 is Running.

If you prefer the unprivileged NGINX image
- Use a public tag that doesn’t require auth, e.g. quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable-alpine (or a specific version), or
- Configure imagePullSecrets for your registry credentials and use your desired private image/tag.

Verify
- kubectl -n debug rollout status deploy/app
- kubectl -n debug get deploy app
- kubectl -n debug get pods -l app=nginx

Want me to switch to a specific image/tag or add imagePullSecrets for a private registry?

outputSteps.content:
I fixed the deployment.

What was wrong
- The image in Deployment debug/app was quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:invalid-tag, which doesn’t exist. Pods were failing with ErrImagePull/ImagePullBackOff.
- My first correction to quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:latest hit 401 UNAUTHORIZED (requires auth).

What I changed
- Updated the deployment to use a public, pullable image:
  - container nginx image: docker.io/library/nginx:alpine

Current status
- Deployment debug/app is healthy and Available=True.
- 1/1 replicas ready; Pod app-59c9bb5f58-mmxh6 is Running.

If you prefer the unprivileged NGINX image
- Use a public tag that doesn’t require auth, e.g. quay.io/nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:stable-alpine (or a specific version), or
- Configure imagePullSecrets for your registry credentials and use your desired private image/tag.

Verify
- kubectl -n debug rollout status deploy/app
- kubectl -n debug get deploy app
- kubectl -n debug get pods -l app=nginx

Want me to switch to a specific image/tag or add imagePullSecrets for a private registry?

Steps.message:
deployment.apps/app condition met
Pod is stable. Verification successful.

results.taskOutput:
I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC (homepage-pvc) couldn’t be bound: “storageclass.storage.k8s.io ‘sc’ not found.”
- Your cluster only has the “standard” StorageClass (rancher.io/local-path).

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe—no data was provisioned yet).
- Recreated homepage-pvc with storageClassName: standard and the same 1Gi request.

Result
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled.
- homepage-pod is now Running in homepage-ns.
  - Node: mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane
  - Pod IP: 10.244.0.29
  - Container nginx: Ready

If you intended to use a different StorageClass name (“sc”), we can create that StorageClass and adjust accordingly.

Steps.message:
namespace/homepage-ns created
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc created
pod/homepage-pod created

I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC (homepage-pvc) couldn’t be bound: “storageclass.storage.k8s.io ‘sc’ not found.”
- Your cluster only has the “standard” StorageClass (rancher.io/local-path).

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe—no data was provisioned yet).
- Recreated homepage-pvc with storageClassName: standard and the same 1Gi request.

Result
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled.
- homepage-pod is now Running in homepage-ns.
  - Node: mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane
  - Pod IP: 10.244.0.29
  - Container nginx: Ready

If you intended to use a different StorageClass name (“sc”), we can create that StorageClass and adjust accordingly.

outputSteps.content:
I found the root cause and fixed it.

What was wrong
- The pod was Pending because its PVC (homepage-pvc) couldn’t be bound: “storageclass.storage.k8s.io ‘sc’ not found.”
- Your cluster only has the “standard” StorageClass (rancher.io/local-path).

What I changed
- Deleted the unbound PVC homepage-pvc (safe—no data was provisioned yet).
- Recreated homepage-pvc with storageClassName: standard and the same 1Gi request.

Result
- The PVC bound successfully and the pod scheduled.
- homepage-pod is now Running in homepage-ns.
  - Node: mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane
  - Pod IP: 10.244.0.29
  - Container nginx: Ready

If you intended to use a different StorageClass name (“sc”), we can create that StorageClass and adjust accordingly.

Steps.message:
Starting verification for fix-pending-pods...
ℹWaiting for PVC 'homepage-pvc' to be 'Bound'...
persistentvolumeclaim/homepage-pvc condition met
'homepage-pvc' is Bound. Verifying that desired state is realized...
Waiting for Pod 'homepage-pod' to be 'Ready'...
pod/homepage-pod condition met
Pod 'homepage-pod' is Ready. Verification successful for .

results.taskOutput:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080 (not 80).  
- The Deployment’s readiness/liveness probes were checking http://:80 on paths /is_ready and /get_status, so the pod never became ready.  
- The Service was targeting port 80, so traffic couldn’t reach the container.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to use port 8080 and path /.
- Added containerPort 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (Service still exposes port 80 inside the cluster).

Current state
- Pod: 1/1 Ready and Running.
- Deployment: Available with 1 replica.
- Service endpoints: point to podIP:8080.
- Verified response from the container returns the NGINX welcome page.

How you can verify
- Inside the cluster: kubectl -n orders run -it --rm curl --image=curlimages/curl -- sh -c "curl -sS webapp:80 | head"
- From your workstation (port-forward): kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (outside the cluster), tell me whether you prefer NodePort or Ingress and I’ll set that up.

Steps.message:
namespace/orders created
deployment.apps/webapp created
service/webapp created
Waiting for pod to start and begin failing health checks...
deployment.apps/webapp condition met
Setup successful: Deployment is no longer available.

I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080 (not 80).  
- The Deployment’s readiness/liveness probes were checking http://:80 on paths /is_ready and /get_status, so the pod never became ready.  
- The Service was targeting port 80, so traffic couldn’t reach the container.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to use port 8080 and path /.
- Added containerPort 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (Service still exposes port 80 inside the cluster).

Current state
- Pod: 1/1 Ready and Running.
- Deployment: Available with 1 replica.
- Service endpoints: point to podIP:8080.
- Verified response from the container returns the NGINX welcome page.

How you can verify
- Inside the cluster: kubectl -n orders run -it --rm curl --image=curlimages/curl -- sh -c "curl -sS webapp:80 | head"
- From your workstation (port-forward): kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (outside the cluster), tell me whether you prefer NodePort or Ingress and I’ll set that up.

rawInput.resource:
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: webapp
  namespace: orders
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: webapp
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: webapp
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: webapp
        image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: 8080
            scheme: HTTP
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3
        livenessProbe:

          timeoutSeconds: 1
          failureThreshold: 3

outputSteps.content:
I found and fixed the issue.

What was wrong
- The image quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged listens on port 8080 (not 80).  
- The Deployment’s readiness/liveness probes were checking http://:80 on paths /is_ready and /get_status, so the pod never became ready.  
- The Service was targeting port 80, so traffic couldn’t reach the container.

What I changed
- Updated the Deployment probes to use port 8080 and path /.
- Added containerPort 8080.
- Updated the Service to targetPort 8080 (Service still exposes port 80 inside the cluster).

Current state
- Pod: 1/1 Ready and Running.
- Deployment: Available with 1 replica.
- Service endpoints: point to podIP:8080.
- Verified response from the container returns the NGINX welcome page.

How you can verify
- Inside the cluster: kubectl -n orders run -it --rm curl --image=curlimages/curl -- sh -c "curl -sS webapp:80 | head"
- From your workstation (port-forward): kubectl -n orders port-forward svc/webapp 8080:80 and open http://localhost:8080

If you need external access (outside the cluster), tell me whether you prefer NodePort or Ingress and I’ll set that up.

Success: Pod is stable with acceptable number of restarts

results.taskOutput:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core API group pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount was forbidden from listing pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- Left the existing apps/deployments list permission as-is.
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding (to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader) already pointed to this Role, so no change needed there.

How to verify
- From an admin context:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods
- If you want to use a ServiceAccount token directly on Kubernetes v1.24+:
  - Create a token: kubectl -n simple-rbac-setup create token pod-reader
  - Use that token in your kubeconfig to list pods in the simple-rbac-setup namespace.

Notes
- This Role grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you would need a ClusterRole and a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

arguments.resource:
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: pod-reader-role
  namespace: simple-rbac-setup
rules:
- apiGroups: [""]
  resources: ["pods"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- apiGroups: ["apps"]
  resources: ["deployments"]
  verbs: ["list"]

Steps.message:
namespace/simple-rbac-setup created
serviceaccount/pod-reader created
role.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-role created
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/pod-reader-binding created

Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core API group pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount was forbidden from listing pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- Left the existing apps/deployments list permission as-is.
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding (to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader) already pointed to this Role, so no change needed there.

How to verify
- From an admin context:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods
- If you want to use a ServiceAccount token directly on Kubernetes v1.24+:
  - Create a token: kubectl -n simple-rbac-setup create token pod-reader
  - Use that token in your kubeconfig to list pods in the simple-rbac-setup namespace.

Notes
- This Role grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you would need a ClusterRole and a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

outputSteps.content:
Root cause
- The Role pod-reader-role in simple-rbac-setup only allowed listing apps/deployments. It did not grant permissions on core API group pods, so the pod-reader ServiceAccount was forbidden from listing pods.

What I changed
- Updated Role simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader-role to include:
  - apiGroup: "" (core)
  - resources: ["pods"]
  - verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
- Left the existing apps/deployments list permission as-is.
- The existing RoleBinding pod-reader-binding (to ServiceAccount simple-rbac-setup/pod-reader) already pointed to this Role, so no change needed there.

How to verify
- From an admin context:
  - kubectl auth can-i --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup list pods
  - kubectl --as=system:serviceaccount:simple-rbac-setup:pod-reader -n simple-rbac-setup get pods
- If you want to use a ServiceAccount token directly on Kubernetes v1.24+:
  - Create a token: kubectl -n simple-rbac-setup create token pod-reader
  - Use that token in your kubeconfig to list pods in the simple-rbac-setup namespace.

Notes
- This Role grants access only within the simple-rbac-setup namespace. If you try to list pods across all namespaces (-A), you would need a ClusterRole and a ClusterRoleBinding instead.

Steps.message: Verification successful: RBAC role correctly reconfigured.

results.taskOutput:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods:
  - Service selector: app=web
  - Deployment/Pods label: app=nginx
  - Result: the Endpoints object had no subsets (no backing pods), so the Service had nothing to route to.
- Nginx in the quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged image listens on port 8080, but the Service targetPort was 80.
  - Result: even with a correct selector, traffic to port 80 wouldn’t reach the container’s port.

What I changed (fix applied)
- Updated the Service to:
  - Select the correct pods: app=nginx
  - Route to the right container port: targetPort=8080
- Final Service spec (key bits):
  - type: ClusterIP
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: name: http, port: 80, targetPort: 8080

Verification
- Endpoints now shows the backing Pod and port 8080.
- Launched a temporary curl pod and confirmed:
  - curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/ returns the default nginx HTML
  - curl http://10.96.219.158/ (ClusterIP) also returns HTML

Why it wasn’t accessible
- Because the Service had no endpoints due to a label mismatch, and it pointed to the wrong targetPort.

Notes and next steps
- Access type: The Service is ClusterIP, so it’s reachable only inside the cluster. If you need external access, choose one:
  - Expose as NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'
  - Or create an Ingress (no Ingress currently exists in web).
- Optional hardening: Add containerPort: 8080 in the Deployment pod spec for clarity (not required for Service functionality).
- I created a temporary pod named curl-test (sleeping). Say the word if you want me to remove it now.

content.text:
NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME        READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE   IP            NODE                             NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS
web         v1           Pod    curl-test   1/1     Running   0          3s    10.244.0.37   mcp-eval-cluster-control-plane   <none>           <none>            <none>

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

---
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

command: curl -sS -m 5 http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/ | head -n2; echo; echo '---'; curl -sS -m 5 http://10.96.219.158/ | head -n2
arguments.name: curl-test

Steps.message:
namespace/web created
deployment.apps/nginx created
service/nginx created

Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods:
  - Service selector: app=web
  - Deployment/Pods label: app=nginx
  - Result: the Endpoints object had no subsets (no backing pods), so the Service had nothing to route to.
- Nginx in the quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged image listens on port 8080, but the Service targetPort was 80.
  - Result: even with a correct selector, traffic to port 80 wouldn’t reach the container’s port.

What I changed (fix applied)
- Updated the Service to:
  - Select the correct pods: app=nginx
  - Route to the right container port: targetPort=8080
- Final Service spec (key bits):
  - type: ClusterIP
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: name: http, port: 80, targetPort: 8080

Verification
- Endpoints now shows the backing Pod and port 8080.
- Launched a temporary curl pod and confirmed:
  - curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/ returns the default nginx HTML
  - curl http://10.96.219.158/ (ClusterIP) also returns HTML

Why it wasn’t accessible
- Because the Service had no endpoints due to a label mismatch, and it pointed to the wrong targetPort.

Notes and next steps
- Access type: The Service is ClusterIP, so it’s reachable only inside the cluster. If you need external access, choose one:
  - Expose as NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'
  - Or create an Ingress (no Ingress currently exists in web).
- Optional hardening: Add containerPort: 8080 in the Deployment pod spec for clarity (not required for Service functionality).
- I created a temporary pod named curl-test (sleeping). Say the word if you want me to remove it now.

outputSteps.content:
Diagnosis
- The Service selector didn’t match the Pods:
  - Service selector: app=web
  - Deployment/Pods label: app=nginx
  - Result: the Endpoints object had no subsets (no backing pods), so the Service had nothing to route to.
- Nginx in the quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged image listens on port 8080, but the Service targetPort was 80.
  - Result: even with a correct selector, traffic to port 80 wouldn’t reach the container’s port.

What I changed (fix applied)
- Updated the Service to:
  - Select the correct pods: app=nginx
  - Route to the right container port: targetPort=8080
- Final Service spec (key bits):
  - type: ClusterIP
  - selector: app=nginx
  - ports: name: http, port: 80, targetPort: 8080

Verification
- Endpoints now shows the backing Pod and port 8080.
- Launched a temporary curl pod and confirmed:
  - curl http://nginx.web.svc.cluster.local/ returns the default nginx HTML
  - curl http://10.96.219.158/ (ClusterIP) also returns HTML

Why it wasn’t accessible
- Because the Service had no endpoints due to a label mismatch, and it pointed to the wrong targetPort.

Notes and next steps
- Access type: The Service is ClusterIP, so it’s reachable only inside the cluster. If you need external access, choose one:
  - Expose as NodePort: kubectl -n web patch svc nginx -p '{"spec":{"type":"NodePort"}}'
  - Or create an Ingress (no Ingress currently exists in web).
- Optional hardening: Add containerPort: 8080 in the Deployment pod spec for clarity (not required for Service functionality).
- I created a temporary pod named curl-test (sleeping). Say the word if you want me to remove it now.

results.taskOutput:
Here’s what was wrong and what I fixed.

Findings
- Pods weren’t running because the Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu, but your cluster has only a control-plane node without that label. The scheduler reported “didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
- Even if pods had scheduled, the Service pointed to targetPort: 80 while the nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080, which would cause connection refused.

Fixes applied
- Patched the Deployment (webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment):
  - Removed the restrictive nodeSelector so it can schedule.
  - Added tolerations for the control-plane taint so it can run on the control-plane node.
  - Exposed container port 8080 with a named port http and added basic readiness/liveness probes on that port.
- Patched the Service (webshop-frontend/web-app-service):
  - Set targetPort: http so it forwards to container port 8080 while keeping service port 80.

Current status
- Pods: 2/2 Running
  - web-app-deployment-559dcc4546-4qqbn (10.244.0.39)
  - web-app-deployment-559dcc4546-vt8xt (10.244.0.40)
- Service Endpoints: both pod IPs are registered; service port 80 -> pod port 8080 (via named port http).

How to verify
- In-cluster test:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run tmp-curl --rm -it --image=busybox:1.36 -- sh
  - Inside: wget -qO- http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local
- Check objects:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get pods -o wide
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get svc web-app-service -o yaml | grep -A3 ports:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get endpoints web-app-service

Notes and next steps
- If you need to keep node placement constraints, either:
  - Add the label environment=production-gpu to a node that should run this app, and then reintroduce the nodeSelector, or
  - Add proper worker nodes and remove the control-plane tolerations.
- If you prefer the app to listen on port 80 inside the container, use an image configured for port 80 and then you can set targetPort: 80; the current setup is correct for nginx-unprivileged (8080).

Let me know if you want me to pin the Deployment to specific labeled nodes or revert the control-plane tolerations once workers are available.

        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:

        ports:

            port: http

        livenessProbe:

            port: http
          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10

kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app-deployment
  namespace: webshop-frontend
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      nodeSelector: {}
      tolerations:
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/control-plane
        operator: Exists
        effect: NoSchedule
      - key: node-role.kubernetes.io/master
        operator: Exists
        effect: NoSchedule
      containers:
      - name: web-app-container

        ports:

          name: http

        readinessProbe:
          httpGet:
            path: /
            port: http
          initialDelaySeconds: 3
          periodSeconds: 5
        livenessProbe:

          initialDelaySeconds: 10
          periodSeconds: 10

outputSteps.content:
Here’s what was wrong and what I fixed.

Findings
- Pods weren’t running because the Deployment had a nodeSelector: environment=production-gpu, but your cluster has only a control-plane node without that label. The scheduler reported “didn’t match Pod’s node affinity/selector.”
- Even if pods had scheduled, the Service pointed to targetPort: 80 while the nginx-unprivileged image listens on 8080, which would cause connection refused.

Fixes applied
- Patched the Deployment (webshop-frontend/web-app-deployment):
  - Removed the restrictive nodeSelector so it can schedule.
  - Added tolerations for the control-plane taint so it can run on the control-plane node.
  - Exposed container port 8080 with a named port http and added basic readiness/liveness probes on that port.
- Patched the Service (webshop-frontend/web-app-service):
  - Set targetPort: http so it forwards to container port 8080 while keeping service port 80.

Current status
- Pods: 2/2 Running
  - web-app-deployment-559dcc4546-4qqbn (10.244.0.39)
  - web-app-deployment-559dcc4546-vt8xt (10.244.0.40)
- Service Endpoints: both pod IPs are registered; service port 80 -> pod port 8080 (via named port http).

How to verify
- In-cluster test:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend run tmp-curl --rm -it --image=busybox:1.36 -- sh
  - Inside: wget -qO- http://web-app-service.webshop-frontend.svc.cluster.local
- Check objects:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get pods -o wide
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get svc web-app-service -o yaml | grep -A3 ports:
  - kubectl -n webshop-frontend get endpoints web-app-service

Notes and next steps
- If you need to keep node placement constraints, either:
  - Add the label environment=production-gpu to a node that should run this app, and then reintroduce the nodeSelector, or
  - Add proper worker nodes and remove the control-plane tolerations.
- If you prefer the app to listen on port 80 inside the container, use an image configured for port 80 and then you can set targetPort: 80; the current setup is correct for nginx-unprivileged (8080).

Let me know if you want me to pin the Deployment to specific labeled nodes or revert the control-plane tolerations once workers are available.

results.taskOutput:
Done. I created the namespace (if missing) and ran the pod as requested.

Current status:
- Namespace: multi-container-logging
- Pod: communication-pod
- Containers:
  - web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
    - readinessProbe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod becomes Ready when web server serves traffic)
  - logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - command: sh -c 'tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log'
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
- Volume: logs-volume (emptyDir)

The pod is Running and Ready.

Manifest applied:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web-server
    image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 2
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
  - name: logger
    image: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: logs-volume
    emptyDir: {}

Current status:
- Namespace: multi-container-logging
- Pod: communication-pod
- Containers:
  - web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
    - readinessProbe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod becomes Ready when web server serves traffic)
  - logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - command: sh -c 'tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log'
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
- Volume: logs-volume (emptyDir)

The pod is Running and Ready.

Manifest applied:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web-server
    image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 2
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
  - name: logger
    image: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: logs-volume
    emptyDir: {}

Verification tips:
- kubectl -n multi-container-logging get pod communication-pod
- kubectl -n multi-container-logging logs communication-pod -c logger --tail=50

outputSteps.content:
Done. I created the namespace (if missing) and ran the pod as requested.

Current status:
- Namespace: multi-container-logging
- Pod: communication-pod
- Containers:
  - web-server: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
    - readinessProbe: HTTP GET / on port 8080 (pod becomes Ready when web server serves traffic)
  - logger: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    - command: sh -c 'tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log'
    - mounts logs-volume at /var/log/nginx
- Volume: logs-volume (emptyDir)

The pod is Running and Ready.

Manifest applied:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: communication-pod
  namespace: multi-container-logging
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web-server
    image: quay.io/nginx/nginx-unprivileged:latest
    ports:
    - containerPort: 8080
      name: http
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
    readinessProbe:
      httpGet:
        path: /
        port: 8080
      initialDelaySeconds: 2
      periodSeconds: 5
      failureThreshold: 3
  - name: logger
    image: quay.io/prometheus/busybox:latest
    command: ["sh", "-c", "tail -f /var/log/nginx/access.log"]
    volumeMounts:
    - name: logs-volume
      mountPath: /var/log/nginx
  volumes:
  - name: logs-volume
    emptyDir: {}

Verification tips:
- kubectl -n multi-container-logging get pod communication-pod
- kubectl -n multi-container-logging logs communication-pod -c logger --tail=50

results.taskOutput:
I resized the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the StorageClass:

- Updated StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path) to allowVolumeExpansion: true
- Patched PVC resize-pv/storage-pvc from 1Gi to 3Gi

Current status:
- PVC spec.requests.storage: 3Gi
- PVC status.capacity.storage is still 1Gi, with event: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

What this means:
- The rancher.io/local-path provisioner does not actually perform volume expansion, so the capacity will not update on this driver even though the request is 3Gi.

Options to actually get 3Gi:
- Create a new 3Gi PVC and migrate data, then point storage-pod at it
  - I can create a new PVC and a temporary helper pod to copy data over (e.g., tar/rsync), then update the pod to use the new claim.
- Switch to a CSI StorageClass that supports expansion, then resize the PVC (and restart the pod to trigger filesystem resize if needed).

Tell me which route you prefer and I’ll proceed.

Steps.message:
namespace/resize-pv created
persistentvolumeclaim/storage-pvc created
pod/storage-pod created

I resized the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the StorageClass:

- Updated StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path) to allowVolumeExpansion: true
- Patched PVC resize-pv/storage-pvc from 1Gi to 3Gi

Current status:
- PVC spec.requests.storage: 3Gi
- PVC status.capacity.storage is still 1Gi, with event: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

What this means:
- The rancher.io/local-path provisioner does not actually perform volume expansion, so the capacity will not update on this driver even though the request is 3Gi.

Options to actually get 3Gi:
- Create a new 3Gi PVC and migrate data, then point storage-pod at it
  - I can create a new PVC and a temporary helper pod to copy data over (e.g., tar/rsync), then update the pod to use the new claim.
- Switch to a CSI StorageClass that supports expansion, then resize the PVC (and restart the pod to trigger filesystem resize if needed).

Tell me which route you prefer and I’ll proceed.

outputSteps.content:
I resized the PVC used by storage-pod to request 3Gi and enabled expansion on the StorageClass:

- Updated StorageClass standard (rancher.io/local-path) to allowVolumeExpansion: true
- Patched PVC resize-pv/storage-pvc from 1Gi to 3Gi

Current status:
- PVC spec.requests.storage: 3Gi
- PVC status.capacity.storage is still 1Gi, with event: “waiting for an external controller to expand this PVC”

What this means:
- The rancher.io/local-path provisioner does not actually perform volume expansion, so the capacity will not update on this driver even though the request is 3Gi.

Options to actually get 3Gi:
- Create a new 3Gi PVC and migrate data, then point storage-pod at it
  - I can create a new PVC and a temporary helper pod to copy data over (e.g., tar/rsync), then update the pod to use the new claim.
- Switch to a CSI StorageClass that supports expansion, then resize the PVC (and restart the pod to trigger filesystem resize if needed).

Tell me which route you prefer and I’ll proceed.

results.taskOutput:
All set. I created the requested namespaces, RBAC, resource quotas, and network policies to provide secure, least-privilege multi-tenancy for alice, bob, and charlie.

What’s been configured

1) Namespaces
- Developer: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod

2) RBAC
- Per-developer full access to own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind:
    - User alice/bob/charlie -> ClusterRole edit (namespace-scoped)
    - Their ServiceAccount (alice-sa/bob-sa/charlie-sa) -> ClusterRole edit (namespace-scoped)
- Read-only access to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding in dev-shared binds:
    - Users alice, bob, charlie -> ClusterRole view
    - ServiceAccounts alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa -> ClusterRole view
- Staging/prod restricted to admins:
  - RoleBindings in staging and prod bind Group cluster-admins -> ClusterRole admin
  - No dev bindings exist in staging/prod, so developers have no access

3) Service Accounts
- alice-sa in dev-alice
- bob-sa in dev-bob
- charlie-sa in dev-charlie
Each SA has:
- Full access in its own namespace (edit)
- Read-only access to dev-shared (view)

4) Resource Quotas
- dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie:
  - CPU: requests 2, limits 2
  - Memory: requests 4Gi, limits 4Gi
  - Pods: 10
  - Services: 5
- dev-shared:
  - CPU: requests 4, limits 4
  - Memory: requests 8Gi, limits 8Gi
  - Pods: 20
  - Services: 10
- staging/prod:
  - CPU: requests 8, limits 8
  - Memory: requests 16Gi, limits 16Gi
  - Pods: 50
  - Services: 20

5) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress + Egress) in: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Allow same-namespace traffic (Ingress + Egress) in all above namespaces
- Developer-to-dev-shared only:
  - Egress from dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie to dev-shared allowed
  - Ingress into dev-shared allowed only from the three developer namespaces
- Cross-developer traffic blocked (no allow rules between dev namespaces)
- DNS access allowed from all above namespaces to kube-dns (kube-system) on TCP/UDP 53
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - No ingress allowed from dev namespaces (default deny in staging/prod + no allow rules)
  - No egress from dev namespaces to staging/prod (egress limited to same-namespace, dev-shared, and DNS)

Notes and next steps
- Users alice, bob, charlie: Ensure your cluster authentication maps these identities (e.g., via OIDC or client certs) so RoleBindings for kind: User take effect.
- Service account tokens (Kubernetes v1.24+): Use kubectl -n <ns> create token <sa> to obtain a short-lived token for CI/CD or automation.
- System services: DNS is permitted. If specific workloads need additional kube-system services, add narrowly scoped allow egress rules to those services and ports only.
- If you also want a default-deny in the default namespace, say the word and I’ll add it; I limited default-deny to the namespaces above to avoid disrupting any existing workloads in default.

If you want, I can verify the policies by launching test pods and demonstrating allowed vs. blocked connectivity.

# Service Accounts for developers in their own namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# RBAC: Full access (edit) in own namespaces for users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-alice-edit
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-bob-edit
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie-edit
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
# RBAC: Read-only (view) access to dev-shared for users and all three SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# ResourceQuotas: developer namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
# ResourceQuota: dev-shared
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-shared
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    limits.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
# ResourceQuotas: staging and prod
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-staging
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-prod
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# NetworkPolicies: default deny (ingress+egress) for all non-system namespaces
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1

  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]

  namespace: dev-shared

  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1

  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
# NetworkPolicies: allow same-namespace traffic in all non-system namespaces
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:

---
# NetworkPolicies: allow dev namespaces to talk to dev-shared (egress only from dev namespaces)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
# NetworkPolicies: dev-shared allows ingress only from dev namespaces (alice/bob/charlie)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1

metadata:
  name: allow-from-dev-namespaces

              - key: env

---
# NetworkPolicies: allow egress to DNS (kube-dns/coredns in kube-system) from all non-system namespaces

Steps.message:
Setting up cluster state for dev environment eval...
Setup completed successfully

All set. I created the requested namespaces, RBAC, resource quotas, and network policies to provide secure, least-privilege multi-tenancy for alice, bob, and charlie.

What’s been configured

1) Namespaces
- Developer: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie
- Shared: dev-shared
- Environments: staging, prod

2) RBAC
- Per-developer full access to own namespace:
  - RoleBindings in dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie bind:
    - User alice/bob/charlie -> ClusterRole edit (namespace-scoped)
    - Their ServiceAccount (alice-sa/bob-sa/charlie-sa) -> ClusterRole edit (namespace-scoped)
- Read-only access to dev-shared:
  - RoleBinding in dev-shared binds:
    - Users alice, bob, charlie -> ClusterRole view
    - ServiceAccounts alice-sa, bob-sa, charlie-sa -> ClusterRole view
- Staging/prod restricted to admins:
  - RoleBindings in staging and prod bind Group cluster-admins -> ClusterRole admin
  - No dev bindings exist in staging/prod, so developers have no access

3) Service Accounts
- alice-sa in dev-alice
- bob-sa in dev-bob
- charlie-sa in dev-charlie
Each SA has:
- Full access in its own namespace (edit)
- Read-only access to dev-shared (view)

4) Resource Quotas
- dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie:
  - CPU: requests 2, limits 2
  - Memory: requests 4Gi, limits 4Gi
  - Pods: 10
  - Services: 5
- dev-shared:
  - CPU: requests 4, limits 4
  - Memory: requests 8Gi, limits 8Gi
  - Pods: 20
  - Services: 10
- staging/prod:
  - CPU: requests 8, limits 8
  - Memory: requests 16Gi, limits 16Gi
  - Pods: 50
  - Services: 20

5) Network Policies and Isolation
- Default deny (Ingress + Egress) in: dev-alice, dev-bob, dev-charlie, dev-shared, staging, prod
- Allow same-namespace traffic (Ingress + Egress) in all above namespaces
- Developer-to-dev-shared only:
  - Egress from dev-alice/dev-bob/dev-charlie to dev-shared allowed
  - Ingress into dev-shared allowed only from the three developer namespaces
- Cross-developer traffic blocked (no allow rules between dev namespaces)
- DNS access allowed from all above namespaces to kube-dns (kube-system) on TCP/UDP 53
- Staging/prod isolation:
  - No ingress allowed from dev namespaces (default deny in staging/prod + no allow rules)
  - No egress from dev namespaces to staging/prod (egress limited to same-namespace, dev-shared, and DNS)

Notes and next steps
- Users alice, bob, charlie: Ensure your cluster authentication maps these identities (e.g., via OIDC or client certs) so RoleBindings for kind: User take effect.
- Service account tokens (Kubernetes v1.24+): Use kubectl -n <ns> create token <sa> to obtain a short-lived token for CI/CD or automation.
- System services: DNS is permitted. If specific workloads need additional kube-system services, add narrowly scoped allow egress rules to those services and ports only.
- If you also want a default-deny in the default namespace, say the word and I’ll add it; I limited default-deny to the namespaces above to avoid disrupting any existing workloads in default.

If you want, I can verify the policies by launching test pods and demonstrating allowed vs. blocked connectivity.

rawInput.resource:
# Service Accounts for developers in their own namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: alice-sa
  namespace: dev-alice
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: bob-sa
  namespace: dev-bob
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: charlie-sa
  namespace: dev-charlie
---
# RBAC: Full access (edit) in own namespaces for users and their SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-alice-edit
  namespace: dev-alice
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-bob-edit
  namespace: dev-bob
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-charlie-edit
  namespace: dev-charlie
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: edit
---
# RBAC: Read-only (view) access to dev-shared for users and all three SAs
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-shared-view
  namespace: dev-shared
subjects:
  - kind: User
    name: alice
  - kind: User
    name: bob
  - kind: User
    name: charlie
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: alice-sa
    namespace: dev-alice
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: bob-sa
    namespace: dev-bob
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: charlie-sa
    namespace: dev-charlie
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: view
---
# ResourceQuotas: developer namespaces
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-dev
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "2"
    limits.cpu: "2"
    requests.memory: 4Gi
    limits.memory: 4Gi
    pods: "10"
    services: "5"
---
# ResourceQuota: dev-shared
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-shared
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    limits.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.memory: 8Gi
    pods: "20"
    services: "10"
---
# ResourceQuotas: staging and prod
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-staging
  namespace: staging
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: rq-prod
  namespace: prod
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "8"
    limits.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "50"
    services: "20"
---
# NetworkPolicies: default deny (ingress+egress) for all non-system namespaces
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-all
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
---
# NetworkPolicies: allow same-namespace traffic in all non-system namespaces
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-same-namespace
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
---
# NetworkPolicies: allow dev namespaces to talk to dev-shared (egress only from dev namespaces)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-to-dev-shared
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              env: dev
              role: shared
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
# NetworkPolicies: dev-shared allows ingress only from dev namespaces (alice/bob/charlie)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-from-dev-namespaces
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  ingress:
    - from:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchExpressions:
              - key: env
                operator: In
                values: ["dev"]
              - key: owner
                operator: In
                values: ["alice", "bob", "charlie"]
  policyTypes: [Ingress]
---
# NetworkPolicies: allow egress to DNS (kube-dns/coredns in kube-system) from all non-system namespaces
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: dev-alice
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: dev-bob
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: dev-charlie
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: dev-shared
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: staging
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-dns
  namespace: prod
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: kube-system
      ports:
        - protocol: UDP
          port: 53
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 53
  policyTypes: [Egress]

# RBAC: Only cluster admins access staging/prod (bind ClusterRole admin to a cluster-admins group)
# Adjust the subject to match your actual admin group/user mapping (e.g., OIDC group name)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: staging-admins
  namespace: staging
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: cluster-admins
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: prod-admins
  namespace: prod
subjects:
  - kind: Group
    name: cluster-admins
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: admin
